content.full_text:
* California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 20, 1990


Peter D. Lemmon
Greve, Clifford, Diepenbrock and Paras
1000 G Street, Suite 400
Post Office Box 2469
Sacramento, CA 95812-2469

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-056

Dear Mr. Lemmon:

This is in response to your request for advice concerning the
permitted uses of campaign funds under the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").1 Because your request does not mention specific
public officials, but rather seeks general advice regarding all
elected officials who are members of the California-Nevada Super
Speed Ground Transportation Commission (hereafter "transportation
commission"), we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(b)(2)(A) (copy enclosed).2

QUESTION

May members of the transportation commission, who are also
elected officials, use campaign funds for travel planned by the
transportation commission including a trip to Germany to inspect
and to evaluate a super speed train?

CONCLUSION

Members of the transportation commission, who are also
elected officials, may use campaign funds for travel provided such
travel is deemed to be an "ordinary and necessary" expense to
accomplish a legislative or governmental purpose. The expense of
a trip to Germany to inspect and evaluate a super speed train is

_____________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear in 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-056
Page 2

an ordinary and necessary expense to accomplish such a purpose.
In certain circumstances, the elected official may be responsible
for the portion of his or her travel expenses outside the United
States which are not attributable to a legislative or governmental
purpose.

FACTS

The California-Nevada Super Speed Ground Transportation
Commission has been established by the Legislatures of California
and Nevada to consider, and if justified, pursue the development
of a super speed ground transportation system connecting Southern
California and Southern Nevada. The transportation commission
shall be composed of a California delegation of eight members
appointed by the Governor, Senate Committee on Rules, and the
Speaker of the Assembly, and a Nevada delegation of eight members
appointed as required by Nevada law. The transportation
commission may accept grants, gifts, fees and allocations from
Nevada, or from other government or private sources. However,
there shall be no cost to the State of California or any of its
political subdivisions.

ANALYSIS

Section 85802 provides in relevant part:

(a) Campaign funds shall not be used to pay
or reimburse the candidate, the elected officer, or
employees or staff of the campaign committee or the
elected officer's governmental agency for travel
expenses and necessary accommodations except when
these expenditures are directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

(1) For the purpose of this section,
payments or reimbursements for travel and
necessary accommodations shall be considered
as directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose if the
payments would meet standards similar to the
standards of the Internal Revenue Service
pursuant to Sections 162 and 274 of the
Internal Revenue Code of deductions of travel
expenses under the federal income tax law.

Section 85802(a)(1).

The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission")
has recently assumed jurisdiction over the laws regarding the
permissible uses of campaign funds. The Commission has not as yet
adopted regulations interpreting the statutory provision quoted
above. Subdivision (a)(1) of Section 85802 permits payments for
travel and accommodations if such payments meet standards similar

Our File No. I-90-056
Page 3

to the standards in the Internal Revenue Code, 26 U.S.C
Sections 162 and 274.

In connection with a trade or business, 26 U.S.C Section 162
permits deductions for all ordinary and necessary expenses paid or
incurred in carrying on such trade or business. The IRS
specifically states that one cannot deduct expenses that are
lavish or extravagant or that are for personal or vacation
purposes.

26 U.S.C Section 274 imposes some limitations on deductions
for travel outside the United States. Furthermore, travel outside
the United States does not include travel from one point in the
United States to another point in the United States. (26 U.S.C
Section 274(c)(3).)

Applying the concepts enunciated in the Internal Revenue Code
provisions, the elected officials may use campaign funds for
travel and accommodations provided such travel and accommodations
are deemed "ordinary and necessary" to accomplish a political,
legislative or governmental purpose.

The transportation commission has been established to
consider the development of a super speed ground transportation
system connecting Southern California and Southern Nevada. Among
the purposes cited for developing such transportation system were:

(1) Provide economic benefits to both
southern California and southern Nevada.

(2) Reduce reliance on gasoline and diesel
fueled engines and encourage the use of alternative
energy sources.

(3) Reduce congestion on Interstate 15
between southern California and Las Vegas.

(4) Provide a working example for a state-of-
the-art transportation system that could play an
essential role in the development of future
commuter service in the Los Angeles basin and the
Las Vegas valley.

(5) Provide quick and convenient
transportation service for residents and visitors
visiting in southern California and southern
Nevada.

In considering the development of the super speed system, the
transportation commission is essentially performing a function
usually performed by a governmental or legislative body in
attempting to provide for the transportation needs of its
residents. In attempting to determine the feasibility of such
super speed system for the benefit of their constituents, elected

Our File No. I-90-056
Page 4

officials may appropriately travel to further the fact-finding
process. Travel to a site in Germany to view a super speed train
appears to be an "ordinary and necessary" expense related to such
determination. Accordingly, travel to Germany would be considered
to be directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose
within the meaning of Section 85802(a).

26 U.S.C 274(c) provides that in the case of certain foreign
travel, expenses not allocable to a trade or business shall not be
deductible. However, this limitation does not apply to travel
which does not exceed one week (seven consecutive days), or to
travel where the portion of the time of travel outside the United
States which is not attributable to the taxpayers trade or
business is less than 25 percent of the total time on such travel.
(26 U.S.C. Section 274 (c)(2)(A) and (B).)

Pending adoption of regulations by the Commission, we shall
follow the standard enunciated in subdivision (c)(2) of 26 U.S.C.
Section 274. Therefore, in the case of foreign travel exceeding
one week, or if the portion of the time of travel outside the
United States which is not attributable to a legislative or
governmental purpose exceeds 25 percent, the elected official may
not use campaign funds for that portion of his or her expenses not
attributable to a legislative or governmental purpose.3

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Jeevan S. Ahuja
Counsel, Legal Division

KED/JSA/aa

Enclosure




3
In the near future the Commission will begin considering and
approving regulations interpreting the statutory provisions
regarding the permissible uses of campaign funds. Therefore, it
is possible that the Commission may adopt regulations in the
future that may be at variance with the advice given in this
letter.

an ordinary and necessary expense to accomplish such a purpose. In certain circumstances, the elected official may be responsible for the portion of his or her travel expenses outside the United States which are not attributable to a legislative or governmental purpose.

sections.analysis:
Section 85802 provides in relevant part:

(a) Campaign funds shall not be used to pay or reimburse the candidate, the elected officer, or employees or staff of the campaign committee or the elected officer's governmental agency for travel expenses and necessary accommodations except when these expenditures are directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

(1) For the purpose of this section, payments or reimbursements for travel and necessary accommodations shall be considered as directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose if the payments would meet standards similar to the standards of the Internal Revenue Service pursuant to Sections 162 and 274 of the Internal Revenue Code of deductions of travel expenses under the federal income tax law.

Section 85802(a)(1).

The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") has recently assumed jurisdiction over the laws regarding the permissible uses of campaign funds. The Commission has not as yet adopted regulations interpreting the statutory provision quoted above. Subdivision (a)(1) of Section 85802 permits payments for travel and accommodations if such payments meet standards similar

to the standards in the Internal Revenue Code, 26 U.S.C Sections 162 and 274.

In connection with a trade or business, 26 U.S.C Section 162 permits deductions for all ordinary and necessary expenses paid or incurred in carrying on such trade or business. The IRS specifically states that one cannot deduct expenses that are lavish or extravagant or that are for personal or vacation purposes.

26 U.S.C Section 274 imposes some limitations on deductions for travel outside the United States. Furthermore, travel outside the United States does not include travel from one point in the United States to another point in the United States. (26 U.S.C. Section 274 (c)(3).)

Applying the concepts enunciated in the Internal Revenue Code provisions, the elected officials may use campaign funds for travel and accommodations provided such travel and accommodations are deemed "ordinary and necessary" to accomplish a political, legislative or governmental purpose.

The transportation commission has been established to consider the development of a super speed ground transportation system connecting Southern California and Southern Nevada. Among the purposes cited for developing such transportation system were:

(1) Provide economic benefits to both southern California and southern Nevada.

(2) Reduce reliance on gasoline and diesel fueled engines and encourage the use of alternative energy sources.

(3) Reduce congestion on Interstate 15 between southern California and Las Vegas.

(4) Provide a working example for a state-of-the-art transportation system that could play an essential role in the development of future commuter service in the Los Angeles basin and the Las Vegas valley.

(5) Provide quick and convenient transportation service for residents and visitors visiting in southern California and southern Nevada.

In considering the development of the super speed system, the transportation commission is essentially performing a function usually performed by a governmental or legislative body in attempting to provide for the transportation needs of its residents. In attempting to determine the feasibility of such super speed system for the benefit of their constituents, elected

Officials may appropriately travel to further the fact-finding process. Travel to a site in Germany to view a super speed train appears to be an "ordinary and necessary" expense related to such determination. Accordingly, travel to Germany would be considered to be directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose within the meaning of Section 85802(a).

26 U.S.C 274(c) provides that in the case of certain foreign travel, expenses not allocable to a trade or business shall not be deductible. However, this limitation does not apply to travel which does not exceed one week (seven consecutive days), or to travel where the portion of the time of travel outside the United States which is not attributable to the taxpayers trade or business is less than 25 percent of the total time on such travel. (26 U.S.C. Section 274 (c)(2)(A) and (B).)

Pending adoption of regulations by the Commission, we shall follow the standard enunciated in subdivision (c)(2) of 26 U.S.C. Section 274. Therefore, in the case of foreign travel exceeding one week, or if the portion of the time of travel outside the United States which is not attributable to a legislative or governmental purpose exceeds 25 percent, the elected official may not use campaign funds for that portion of his or her expenses not attributable to a legislative or governmental purpose.3

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you requested. If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

an ordinary and necessary expense to accomplish such a purpose. In certain circumstances, the elected official may be responsible for the portion of his or her travel expenses outside the United States which are not attributable to a legislative or governmental purpose.
